title: Chapter 6 – Overview of Accounting for Assets
description: Examines the conceptual framework for asset definition, recognition, measurement, and the specific accounting standards (LKAS/SLFRS) for various asset categories.
subject: ACC1370 - Financial Accounting and Reporting

questions.question: An entity possesses a highly specialized machine designed exclusively for a specific process, leased under a short-term, cancellable operating lease. Applying the IASB Conceptual Framework, which asset characteristic is most likely not met, challenging its recognition as an asset by the lessee?
questions.rationale: Control implies the ability to direct the use of the resource and obtain the economic benefits. A short-term, cancellable operating lease generally means the entity cannot unilaterally prevent others from using the asset or fully direct its use, challenging the control element.

questions.question: According to the IASB Conceptual Framework, which factor dictates when an item meeting the definition of an asset should be recognized in the financial statements?
options: When the fair value can be determined reliably
When management formally intends to use the asset for more than one period
When recognition provides useful information (relevance and faithful representation)
When it is probable that future economic benefits will flow to the entity
questions.answer: When recognition provides useful information (relevance and faithful representation)
questions.rationale: The recognition criteria in the IASB Conceptual Framework explicitly state that an item meeting the definition of an asset should only be recognized if such recognition provides users with useful information, i.e., relevant information and a faithful representation.

questions.question: Which of the following measurement bases, identified in the IASB Conceptual Framework, represents the current market-based exit price for an asset, without considering its specific use by the entity?
questions.rationale: Fair Value is a current value measurement that reflects a market participant's perspective (exit price) and does not depend on the entity's specific planned use. Value in Use is also a current value measure but is entity-specific.

questions.question: LKAS 01 generally requires a Current/Non-current classification for assets. Under what specific condition does LKAS 01 allow an entity to present all assets in order of liquidity instead?
options: When the entity has no current liabilities
If the entity operates in a highly cyclical industry
When a presentation based on liquidity provides information that is reliable and more relevant
If the entity adopts the Cost Model for all non-current assets
questions.answer: When a presentation based on liquidity provides information that is reliable and more relevant
questions.rationale: Paragraph 60 of LKAS 01 provides an exception to the current/non-current distinction, permitting presentation in order of liquidity if that approach provides information that is reliable and more relevant.

questions.question: A property is held by a company for capital appreciation. The company's staff are located on the ground floor to manage the property, occupying less than 5% of the total floor space. How should this asset be classified?
questions.rationale: Investment property is held for capital appreciation or rentals, rather than use in production or administration. Since the administrative use (managing the property) is incidental and occupies a minor portion, the property is classified as Investment Property.

questions.question: In a tea plantation, the tea bushes themselves are accounted for under LKAS 16 (PPE) and are considered 'bearer plants'. What is the accounting treatment for the tea leaves after they have been picked (harvested)?
options: Measured at fair value less cost to sell under LKAS 41
Measured at the lower of cost and net realisable value under LKAS 2
Measured at fair value through profit or loss under SLFRS 9
questions.answer: Measured at the lower of cost and net realisable value under LKAS 2
questions.rationale: The harvested produce (tea leaves) is measured at fair value less cost to sell at the point of harvest under LKAS 41. However, once harvested, it becomes a product resulting from processing after harvest and is accounted for as Inventory (LKAS 2), measured at the lower of cost and net realisable value.

questions.question: A company classifies a piece of machinery as a Non-current Asset Held for Sale. Its carrying amount is Rs. 10 million. Its Fair Value is Rs. 12 million, and the Cost to Sell is estimated at Rs. 1 million. What is the amount at which the asset should be measured immediately after classification?
questions.rationale: Non-current assets classified as held for sale are measured at the lower of their carrying amount (Rs. 10 million) or fair value less cost to sell (Rs. 12 million - Rs. 1 million = Rs. 11 million). Since Rs. 10 million is lower, that is the measurement amount.

questions.question: An entity has a brand name with high public recognition. It cannot reliably measure the brand's cost, although future economic benefits are probable. Which LKAS 38 recognition condition is not met, preventing its recognition as an Intangible Asset?
options: It is an identifiable non-monetary asset
It is probable that future economic benefits will flow to the entity
The cost of the asset can be measured reliably
questions.answer: The cost of the asset can be measured reliably
questions.rationale: LKAS 38 requires two recognition criteria: (1) probable future economic benefits and (2) reliable measurement of cost. If the cost cannot be reliably measured, the second criterion is failed.

questions.question: Under SLFRS 9, a financial asset measured at 'Fair Value Through Other Comprehensive Income' (FVOCI) implies a business model where the asset is held for what dual purpose?
options: Collecting contractual cash flows and selling the financial asset
To achieve joint control over the investee and to collect dividends
Collecting contractual cash flows or for immediate sale
questions.answer: Collecting contractual cash flows and selling the financial asset
questions.rationale: SLFRS 9 classifies debt instruments based on the entity's business model. The FVOCI category is used when the business model objective is achieved by both collecting contractual cash flows and selling financial assets.

questions.question: Which level of influence, defined by LKAS 28, gives an investor the power to participate in the financial and operating policy decisions of the investee, but is not control or joint control?
questions.rationale: LKAS 28 defines an associate as an entity over which the investor has significant influence. Significant influence is the power to participate in policy decisions but is not control or joint control.

questions.question: A lessee's Right-of-Use (ROU) Asset relates to a class of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) to which the lessee applies the Revaluation Model in LKAS 16. What measurement model can the lessee elect to apply to the ROU Asset?
questions.rationale: SLFRS 16 allows a lessee to elect to apply the LKAS 16 revaluation model to ROU assets if they relate to a class of PPE to which the lessee already applies that revaluation model.

questions.question: Under SLFRS 11, a joint arrangement where the parties with joint control have rights to the net assets of the arrangement is classified as a:
questions.rationale: A Joint Venture is a joint arrangement where the parties have rights to the net assets of the arrangement. A Joint Operation is where the parties have rights to the assets and obligations for the liabilities relating to the arrangement.

questions.question: What is the key distinguishing feature of 'Value in Use' as a measurement base, compared to 'Fair Value'?
options: Value in Use is always higher than Fair Value.
Fair Value considers the entity's specific planned use, while Value in Use does not.
Value in Use is an entity-specific value, reflecting its future cash flows from use.
Value in Use is an entry price, while Fair Value is an exit price.
questions.answer: Value in Use is an entity-specific value, reflecting its future cash flows from use.
questions.rationale: Value in Use is based on the cash flows an entity expects to derive from the asset, making it entity-specific. Fair Value is a market-based measurement.

questions.question: An investment in debentures held till maturity, which satisfies the 'solely payments of principal and interest' cash flow test, is most likely measured under which SLFRS 9 category?
options: Financial assets at fair value through profit or loss
Financial assets at fair value through other comprehensive income
questions.rationale: Financial assets held under a business model with the objective to collect contractual cash flows, and whose contractual cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest, are measured at Amortised Cost.

questions.question: A manufacturer is determining the value of its unsold stock (Inventories). Which principle, specific to Inventories (LKAS 2), requires the asset to be reported at a measure that reflects prudence in asset valuation?
options: Measured at Fair Value less Cost to Sell
Measured at the lower of Cost and Net Realisable Value
questions.answer: Measured at the lower of Cost and Net Realisable Value
questions.rationale: LKAS 2 mandates that inventories be measured at the lower of cost and net realisable value. This ensures assets are not overstated, reflecting prudence.

questions.question: Subsequent to initial recognition, Investment Property is measured using the cost model or the [dropdown_1] model, while Property, Plant and Equipment is measured using the cost model or the [dropdown_2] model.
questions.rationale: LKAS 40 allows the Cost Model or Fair Value Model for Investment Property. LKAS 16 allows the Cost Model or Revaluation Model for Property, Plant and Equipment.

questions.question: An entity classifies an asset as current if it expects to realise the asset within [dropdown_1] months, or if it holds the asset primarily for the purpose of [dropdown_2] in the ordinary course of business.
questions.rationale: An asset is classified as current if it is expected to be realised in 12 months, or is held primarily for trading.

questions.question: The IASB Conceptual Framework defines an asset as a present economic resource which could be identified as a [dropdown_1] that has the potential to produce [dropdown_2].
questions.rationale: An economic resource is defined as a 'right that has the potential to produce economic benefits'.

questions.question: LKAS 38 defines an intangible asset as an identifiable non-monetary asset without physical ________.
questions.rationale: The key defining characteristic of an intangible asset in LKAS 38 is that it is without physical substance.

questions.question: Biological assets covered by LKAS 41 are generally measured at fair value less ________ to sell at initial recognition.
questions.rationale: Biological assets and agricultural produce at the point of harvest are measured at fair value less cost to sell.

questions.question: The accounting for Investment in Subsidiaries is primarily governed by the requirements of ________, which requires the preparation of consolidated financial statements.
questions.rationale: SLFRS 10 deals with Consolidated Financial Statements, establishing the principles for the presentation and preparation of consolidated financial statements when an entity controls one or more other entities (subsidiaries).

questions.question: Select all conditions that must be met for a non-current asset to be classified as 'Held for Sale' under SLFRS 5.
options: Its carrying amount must be recovered principally through a sales transaction.
It must be expected to be sold within twelve months from the date of classification.
It must be available for immediate sale in its present condition.
The sale must be highly probable.
answer: Its carrying amount must be recovered principally through a sales transaction.
It must be available for immediate sale in its present condition.
The sale must be highly probable.
questions.rationale: SLFRS 5 criteria for classification as Held for Sale include recovery principally through a sales transaction, being available for immediate sale in its present condition, and the sale being highly probable.

questions.question: Which of the following items meet the definition of a Financial Asset under LKAS 32 and SLFRS 9?
options: An investment in the equity instrument of another entity.
A contractual right to receive cash from another entity (e.g., Trade Receivables).
Inventories held for sale in the ordinary course of business.
Cash on hand and demand deposits.
answer: An investment in the equity instrument of another entity.
A contractual right to receive cash from another entity (e.g., Trade Receivables).
Cash on hand and demand deposits.
questions.rationale: Financial assets include cash, an equity instrument of another entity, or a contractual right to receive cash/another financial asset. Inventories are a non-financial asset.

questions.question: According to the classification examples in LKAS 01, which of the following are typically classified as Non-current Assets?
questions.rationale: Biological Assets, Investment Property, Investment in Subsidiaries, and Investment in Associates are examples of Non-current assets. Inventories and Trade/Other Receivables are examples of Current assets.

questions.question: Match the Sri Lanka Accounting Standard (LKAS/SLFRS) to the primary asset it governs.

matchPairs.right: Property held for rentals or capital appreciation
A lessee's right to use an underlying asset

questions.question: Match the measurement basis to its conceptual definition.

matchPairs.right: The cost incurred to acquire the asset
The cost of an equivalent asset at the measurement date
Entity-specific value of future cash flows from use

questions.question: Match the asset category to its primary classification regarding physical substance.

Critically analyze the statement: 'An item must be recognized in the financial statements if it meets the three defining characteristics of an asset (present economic resource, control, past event)'. Explain how the IASB Conceptual Framework's recognition criteria qualify or restrict this assumption, focusing on the concepts of relevance and faithful representation.

A fruit farm has a field of orange trees and a warehouse full of harvested, bagged oranges. Explain the fundamental conceptual difference between the tree and the bagged oranges and describe the point in the asset life-cycle (based on LKAS 41 and LKAS 2) where the accounting standard and measurement basis fundamentally change.

Compare and contrast the effect of selecting the Fair Value Model (LKAS 40) versus the Cost Model (LKAS 40) for subsequent measurement of Investment Property on an entity's Statement of Financial Position and Statement(s) of Financial Performance. Specifically address the impact on asset carrying value and the recognition of gains/losses.
